# Defending the Global Human Rights System from Authoritarian Assault: How Democracies Can Retake the Initiative

### [TWEET](https://ctt.ac/m0ae9) | [SHARE](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https%3A//www.ned.org/defending-the-global-human-rights-system-from-authoritarian-assault-how-democracies-can-retake-the-initiative/)

![]()

### [DOWNLOAD THE REPORT](https://www.ned.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/NED_FORUM-Countering-Authoritarian-Efforts-Report_Final.pdf)

**Authoritarian influence in multilateral institutions** is growing rapidly and poses a serious threat to democratic and human rights principles. Repressive governments have worked to undermine mechanisms that are meant to ensure accountability for human rights abuses and to transform the United Nations, its related bodies, and other international institutions into fora for mutual praise. Both the Chinese Communist Party and the Kremlin are working to subvert human rights norms on the international stage, peddle favorable narratives, and oppose resolutions examining their poor human rights records. Democratic societies must rally behind the global human rights system and ensure that it remains capable of assisting activists and victims around the world. 

In a new **report** for the International Forum for Democratic Studies, **Dr. Rana Siu Inboden**, a senior fellow with the Robert S. Strauss Center for International Security and Law at The University of Texas at Austin, examines how **authoritarian regimes exploit multilateral institutions to further their illiberal goals** and how **democracies can work together to uphold the system and hold abusers accountable**.

**On Monday, July 10,** the International Forum hosted a public event marking the launch of this report, featuring the report author. Click [**here**](https://bit.ly/3D5B03a) to view the event recording.

**In an exclusive article** for [Just Security](https://www.justsecurity.org/87190/going-on-offense-against-authoritarians-at-the-un-human-rights-council-and-beyond/), report author Rana Siu Inboden answers the question: How can democracies go on offense against authoritarian powers bent on hollowing out the global human rights system, including the UNHRC? Read the article [here](https://www.justsecurity.org/87190/going-on-offense-against-authoritarians-at-the-un-human-rights-council-and-beyond/)!

# RESPONDING TO AUTHORITARIAN INFLUENCE

It is in the vital interest of democratic societies to rally behind the global human rights system and ensure that it remains capable of assisting activists and victims around the world, even in the most repressive environments. A robust response from democracies could be built around the following steps: 

Without a vigorous democratic response, PRC, Kremlin, and other authoritarian influence in multilateral institutions is likely to grow significantly, and LMG arguments could persuade an increasing number of countries to join in the debilitation of the international human rights system. The UN human rights system is worth defending because of the moral weight it carries, the accountability it provides for repressive governments, and its ability to inspire local activists. By taking the initiative, competing for positions in multilateral bodies, forging coalitions across regions and development levels, cultivating civil society networks, and investing in long-term diplomatic campaigns, democratic states would dramatically improve the outlook for global human rights mechanisms and for the expansion of human freedom.

[***Defending the Global Human Rights System from Authoritarian Assault: How Democracies Can Retake the Initiative***](https://www.ned.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/NED_FORUM-Countering-Authoritarian-Efforts-Report_Final.pdf) by Dr. Rana Siu Inboden

# About The Author

**Dr. Rana Siu Inboden** is a senior fellow with the Robert S. Strauss Center for International Security and Law at The University of Texas at Austin. She serves as a consultant on human rights, democracy and rule of law projects in Asia for a number of nongovernmental organizations and conducts research related to international human rights, Chinese foreign policy, the effectiveness of international human rights and democracy projects and authoritarian collaboration in the United Nations. Her first book, *China and the International Human Rights Regime* (Cambridge, 2021) examines China’s role in the international human rights regime between 1982 and 2017. Dr. Inboden holds a DPhil from the Department of Politics and International Relations at Oxford University.

# With Rising Authoritarianism, Promoting, Respecting Human Rights More Important Than Ever, Secretary-General Stresses in Message on International Day

Following is UN Secretary-General António Guterres’ message for Human Rights Day, observed on 10 December:

“All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights.”

The iconic opening sentence of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights is as important today as it was when it was adopted 75 years ago.

The Universal Declaration is a roadmap, helping to end wars, heal divisions and promote lives of peace and dignity for all.

But the world is losing its way.  Conflicts are raging.  Poverty and hunger are increasing.  Inequalities are deepening.  The climate crisis is a human rights crisis that is hitting the most vulnerable hardest.

Authoritarianism is on the rise.  Civic space is shrinking and the media is under attack from all sides.  Gender equality remains a distant dream and women’s reproductive rights are being rolled back.

Today, it is more important than ever to promote and respect all human rights — social, cultural, economic, civil and political — which protect us all.  The Universal Declaration shows the way to common values and approaches that can help resolve tensions and create the security and stability our world craves.

As we work to update global frameworks and make them more effective in the twenty-first century, human rights must have a unique and central role.  I call on Member States to use this seventy-fifth anniversary, and the Summit of the Future next year, to strengthen their commitment to the timeless values of the Universal Declaration.

And on Human Rights Day, I urge people around the world to promote and respect human rights, every day, for everyone, everywhere.

The Struggle for Human Rights in Authoritarian Regimes
======================================================

*   ![Image 2: Writer: Edmarverson A. Santos](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/aa0720_221a4d10972142cbb19e9f9c0a9a8de4%7Emv2.png/v1/fill/w_32,h_32,al_c,q_85,enc_avif,quality_auto/aa0720_221a4d10972142cbb19e9f9c0a9a8de4%7Emv2.png) Edmarverson A. Santos
*   Aug 5, 2024
*   20 min read

I. Introduction
---------------

### Definition of Authoritarian Regimes

Authoritarian regimes are political systems characterized by the concentration of power in a single authority or a small group, often maintained through repressive and undemocratic means. These regimes typically lack the institutional structures that support checks and balances, resulting in limited political pluralism and constrained civil liberties. Unlike democratic systems, which are based on free and fair elections, separation of powers, and respect for individual rights, authoritarian regimes often manipulate or completely bypass electoral processes to maintain their grip on power.

Several typologies of authoritarian regimes exist, each with distinct features and methods of control. For instance, personalist regimes revolve around a single leader whose authority is not subject to significant institutional constraints. Military regimes, on the other hand, are governed by a coalition of military officers, while single-party states are dominated by a single political party that restricts opposition. Understanding these distinctions is crucial for comprehending how authoritarian regimes operate and maintain control over their populations.

### Importance of Human Rights

Human rights are fundamental freedoms and protections to which all individuals are entitled, regardless of nationality, ethnicity, or any other status. These rights are enshrined in the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR), adopted by the United Nations General Assembly in 1948. The UDHR outlines a broad spectrum of rights, including civil, political, economic, social, and cultural rights, serving as a universal standard for human dignity and equality.

The significance of human rights lies in their role as a foundation for justice, peace, and the protection of human dignity. They provide a benchmark against which the actions of states, particularly authoritarian regimes, can be measured. The pursuit of human rights ensures that individuals can live free from fear and want, participate fully in societal life, and enjoy the benefits of their labor and creativity.

Authoritarian regimes often violate these fundamental rights to maintain control and suppress dissent. The denial of basic freedoms, such as freedom of speech, assembly, and the press, is a common feature in these regimes. Arbitrary arrests, torture, and extrajudicial killings further underscore the critical need for a robust international human rights framework to hold these regimes accountable.

The struggle for human rights in authoritarian regimes is not merely a legal or political issue but a profound ethical challenge. It demands the concerted effort of international organizations, governments, and civil society to promote and protect these rights.

II. The Nature of Authoritarian Regimes
---------------------------------------

### Political Structure and Power Dynamics

Authoritarian regimes are defined by their concentration of power in a single entity or a small group, bypassing the typical checks and balances found in democratic systems. The centralization of authority is a key characteristic, allowing the ruling entity to control all aspects of governance without meaningful opposition. The leadership in these regimes often relies on a combination of coercion, co-optation, and patronage to maintain power and suppress dissent.

**Types of Authoritarian Regimes**

1.   **Personalist Regimes**: In these systems, power is centralized around a single leader who often enjoys unchecked authority. Examples include North Korea under Kim Jong-un and Zimbabwe under Robert Mugabe. These leaders typically employ extensive surveillance and repression to eliminate opposition.

2.   **Military Regimes**: Here, the military controls the government, either directly or through a civilian façade. Examples include Myanmar and Egypt. Military regimes justify their rule by claiming to provide stability and security, often at the expense of civil liberties.

3.   **Single-Party States**: Dominated by one political party that monopolizes political power, these states often suppress opposition parties and restrict political competition. China under the Communist Party and Cuba under the Communist Party of Cuba are prime examples.

4.   **Hybrid Regimes**: These systems blend elements of authoritarianism with nominal democratic features, such as elections that are neither free nor fair. Russia under Vladimir Putin and Turkey under Recep Tayyip Erdoğan exemplify this category, where democratic institutions exist but are manipulated to maintain authoritarian control.

### Methods of Control and Repression

Authoritarian regimes employ a variety of methods to maintain control and suppress opposition. These methods can be broadly categorized into surveillance, censorship, propaganda, and the use of violence and intimidation.

**Surveillance**: Extensive surveillance is a hallmark of authoritarian regimes. This includes monitoring communications, tracking movements, and gathering intelligence on dissidents. The use of advanced technology, such as facial recognition and internet monitoring, has enhanced the ability of regimes to surveil their populations. China’s surveillance state, exemplified by its Social Credit System, is a prominent example.

**Censorship**: Control over information is crucial for authoritarian regimes. This involves restricting access to information, censoring media, and suppressing free speech. Independent media are often co-opted or shut down, and journalists face harassment, imprisonment, or worse. North Korea’s strict control over all media content illustrates extreme censorship practices.

**Propaganda**: Propaganda is used to shape public perception and maintain regime legitimacy. State-controlled media disseminate information that glorifies the regime and demonizes opponents. Educational systems are often manipulated to indoctrinate citizens from a young age. The former Soviet Union's use of propaganda to promote communist ideology is a classic example.

**Violence and Intimidation**: Authoritarian regimes frequently resort to violence to maintain control. This includes the use of security forces to disperse protests, arbitrary arrests, torture, and extrajudicial killings. The use of paramilitary groups and secret police to intimidate and eliminate opposition is also common. The Syrian regime’s brutal crackdown on dissent during the civil war highlights the extreme use of violence and intimidation.

### Legitimation Strategies

Despite their coercive nature, authoritarian regimes often seek to legitimize their rule through various strategies. These strategies help to maintain public support or at least acquiescence, mitigating the need for constant repression.

**Economic Performance and Public Goods Provision**: Many authoritarian regimes derive legitimacy from their ability to deliver economic growth and provide public goods. By improving living standards and offering social services, regimes can garner public support. China’s economic boom under the Chinese Communist Party has been a significant source of its legitimacy.

**Nationalism and Historical Narratives**: Authoritarian regimes often invoke nationalism and historical narratives to legitimize their rule. This can involve emphasizing past glories, external threats, and national unity. Russia under Putin, for instance, has used nationalist rhetoric and references to historical events like World War II to strengthen his regime’s legitimacy.

**Institutionalization and Legal Rationality**: Some authoritarian regimes create the semblance of legal-rational authority by establishing formal institutions and legal frameworks that appear to follow rule-of-law principles. This can include holding elections, albeit manipulated, and having a constitution that is selectively enforced. Singapore, under the People’s Action Party, has used such strategies to maintain a stable and economically successful authoritarian regime.

III. Human Rights Violations in Authoritarian Regimes
-----------------------------------------------------

### Civil and Political Rights

**Suppression of Freedom of Speech, Assembly, and Association**

In authoritarian regimes, the suppression of civil and political rights is a pervasive practice aimed at maintaining control and silencing dissent. Freedom of speech is frequently curtailed through stringent laws that criminalize criticism of the government. For example, in China, the government employs broad laws against "subversion of state power" to detain activists, journalists, and academics who express dissenting views. The Great Firewall of China restricts access to foreign news websites and social media platforms, effectively stifling free expression online.

Freedom of assembly and association is similarly repressed. Authoritarian regimes often prohibit public gatherings and demonstrations, deeming them threats to national security or public order. In Russia, laws regulating public assembly are used to prevent opposition rallies, and participants in unauthorized protests face arrest and harsh penalties. The Russian government's crackdown on non-governmental organizations (NGOs), through the “foreign agents” law, restricts their ability to operate freely and independently.

**Arbitrary Arrests, Detentions, and Torture**

Arbitrary arrests and detentions are common tools used by authoritarian regimes to eliminate political opposition and instill fear among the populace. These practices are often justified under the guise of national security or counterterrorism. In Egypt, the government has used emergency laws to detain thousands of political opponents, activists, and journalists without due process. Many detainees are held incommunicado, denied legal representation, and subjected to prolonged pretrial detention.

Torture and other forms of ill-treatment are systematically used in many authoritarian regimes to extract confessions, punish dissidents, and intimidate others. Reports from organizations such as Amnesty International and Human Rights Watch document widespread torture in countries like Syria, where detainees are subjected to severe beatings, electric shocks, and sexual violence. The infamous Saydnaya prison in Syria has been described as a "human slaughterhouse" due to the extreme torture and extrajudicial executions occurring there.

### Socioeconomic Rights

**Inequality in Resource Distribution**

Authoritarian regimes often exacerbate socioeconomic inequalities through policies that favor the elite and marginalize the poor. Corruption and nepotism are rampant, with state resources disproportionately allocated to those with political connections. In countries like Venezuela, the government’s control over the economy has led to widespread corruption, resulting in severe shortages of food, medicine, and basic necessities. The mismanagement of resources and prioritization of loyalty over competence have plunged millions into poverty.

**Limited Access to Healthcare, Education, and Employment**

Socioeconomic rights, including access to healthcare, education, and employment, are often neglected in authoritarian regimes. State spending on public services is typically low, and what is available is often of poor quality and unequally distributed. In North Korea, the regime’s focus on military expenditure and political loyalty results in chronic food insecurity and inadequate healthcare for the general population. The lack of access to quality education further entrenches poverty, as citizens are deprived of the skills needed to improve their socio-economic status.

In many authoritarian states, employment opportunities are closely tied to political loyalty. In Zimbabwe, during Robert Mugabe's rule, job opportunities in the public sector were often reserved for members of the ruling party, ZANU-PF. This patronage system not only limits economic mobility but also reinforces the regime’s control by making employment contingent on political support.

### Case Studies

**North Korea**

North Korea is one of the most repressive regimes in the world, with widespread and systematic human rights abuses. The government, under the leadership of Kim Jong-un, exercises total control over all aspects of life, including the economy, media, and personal freedoms. Arbitrary detention, forced labor, and public executions are common. The regime uses fear and punishment to maintain its grip on power, and the populace is subjected to pervasive surveillance and indoctrination.

**China**

China’s human rights record, particularly concerning ethnic minorities, is increasingly scrutinized. The Chinese government’s treatment of Uighur Muslims in Xinjiang has been condemned globally. Reports indicate that over a million Uighurs have been detained in so-called "re-education camps," where they are subjected to forced labor, indoctrination, and abuse. Additionally, the crackdown on pro-democracy activists in Hong Kong, involving mass arrests and the imposition of the National Security Law, highlights the regime's determination to suppress dissent.

**Saudi Arabia**

Saudi Arabia, under the leadership of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, has seen a combination of economic reforms and severe repression. The government’s crackdown on dissent includes the arrest of activists, clerics, and journalists. The murder of journalist Jamal Khashoggi in the Saudi consulate in Istanbul brought international condemnation and highlighted the lengths to which the regime will go to silence critics. Women’s rights activists have been detained and reportedly tortured for campaigning against the male guardianship system and for the right to drive.

IV. International Response and Advocacy
---------------------------------------

### Role of International Organizations

International organizations play a pivotal role in monitoring, documenting, and advocating against human rights violations in authoritarian regimes. These entities provide platforms for global cooperation and pressure, aiming to uphold international human rights standards.

**United Nations**

The United Nations (UN) is at the forefront of international human rights advocacy. Various UN bodies, such as the Human Rights Council (HRC) and the Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights (OHCHR), are instrumental in addressing human rights abuses. The HRC conducts Universal Periodic Reviews (UPRs) to assess human rights practices in all member states, including authoritarian regimes. These reviews provide a forum for holding states accountable and recommending improvements.

Special rapporteurs and independent experts appointed by the UN investigate specific human rights issues or situations in particular countries. Their reports bring international attention to abuses and often serve as the basis for advocacy and action. For example, the UN Special Rapporteur on North Korea has documented extensive human rights violations, prompting international condemnation and calls for accountability.

**Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs)**

International NGOs, such as Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch, and Freedom House, play a critical role in documenting human rights abuses, advocating for victims, and pressuring governments to uphold human rights standards. These organizations conduct field research, publish detailed reports, and launch global campaigns to raise awareness and mobilize public opinion.

Amnesty International’s annual reports provide comprehensive overviews of the human rights situation in countries worldwide, including authoritarian regimes. Human Rights Watch’s investigations and reports on countries like Syria, China, and Saudi Arabia have highlighted abuses and contributed to international advocacy efforts.

### Diplomatic and Economic Sanctions

Diplomatic and economic sanctions are key tools used by the international community to pressure authoritarian regimes into respecting human rights. These measures can target specific individuals or entities responsible for abuses, as well as broader sectors of the economy.

**Types of Sanctions**

1.   **Targeted Sanctions**: These include asset freezes, travel bans, and restrictions on financial transactions against specific individuals or entities. For example, the United States and the European Union have imposed targeted sanctions on Russian officials and oligarchs involved in human rights abuses and the annexation of Crimea.

2.   **Economic Sanctions**: Broader sanctions can be imposed on sectors of an economy, such as trade restrictions, arms embargoes, and financial prohibitions. Comprehensive sanctions against Iran, for instance, aim to pressure the regime over its human rights record and nuclear program.

**Effectiveness and Limitations**

Sanctions can be effective in signaling international disapproval and isolating authoritarian regimes. They can disrupt the economic interests of ruling elites and create incentives for policy changes. However, sanctions also have limitations. They can lead to unintended consequences, such as economic hardship for the general population, and may strengthen regime propaganda that blames external actors for internal problems.

The effectiveness of sanctions often depends on international coordination and enforcement. Unilateral sanctions may have limited impact if other countries continue to engage with the targeted regime. Multilateral sanctions, coordinated by bodies like the UN or regional organizations, are generally more effective in exerting pressure.

### Transnational Advocacy Networks

Transnational advocacy networks (TANs) consist of diverse actors, including NGOs, social movements, international organizations, and sympathetic governments, working together to promote human rights. These networks leverage global communication tools to coordinate actions, share information, and mobilize support.

**Global and Local Activism**

TANs facilitate collaboration between global and local activists, amplifying the voices of those on the ground and providing them with resources and international platforms. For instance, the campaign to end apartheid in South Africa involved extensive international advocacy, including sanctions, divestment, and public awareness campaigns, coordinated by a network of global and local activists.

**Social Media and Digital Communication**

The rise of social media and digital communication has revolutionized transnational advocacy. Platforms like Twitter, Facebook, and WhatsApp enable rapid information sharing, coordination of protests, and global solidarity campaigns. The Arab Spring uprisings demonstrated the power of digital tools in mobilizing mass protests and drawing international attention to human rights abuses in authoritarian regimes.

**Case Study: Hong Kong Protests**

The pro-democracy protests in Hong Kong exemplify the impact of transnational advocacy networks. Local activists have used social media to document police brutality and human rights violations, garnering international support and solidarity. Global advocacy groups have amplified these efforts, pressuring foreign governments to condemn the Chinese government’s actions and support the protesters’ demands.

V. Mechanisms of Resistance and Change
--------------------------------------

### Grassroots Movements

Grassroots movements are pivotal in challenging authoritarian regimes and advocating for human rights. These movements often emerge organically from within the affected population and rely on widespread participation to exert pressure on authoritarian governments.

**Nonviolent Protests and Civil Disobedience**

Nonviolent protests and civil disobedience are common strategies employed by grassroots movements. These tactics aim to draw international attention and moral support while minimizing the risk of violent repression. Historical examples include Mahatma Gandhi's campaigns for Indian independence and the civil rights movement led by Martin Luther King Jr. in the United States.

In authoritarian contexts, nonviolent movements have been instrumental in significant political changes. The People Power Revolution in the Philippines, which led to the ousting of Ferdinand Marcos in 1986, demonstrated the power of sustained, nonviolent protest. More recently, the Sudanese Revolution of 2019 showcased how persistent, peaceful demonstrations could lead to the toppling of a long-standing authoritarian regime.

**Role of Women and Marginalized Groups**

Women and marginalized groups often play crucial roles in grassroots movements. Their involvement brings diverse perspectives and strengthens the legitimacy and reach of the resistance. For instance, in Iran, women have been at the forefront of protests against mandatory veiling laws, challenging broader gender discrimination and human rights abuses.

Marginalized groups, including ethnic and religious minorities, also contribute significantly to resistance efforts. The role of ethnic minority groups in Myanmar, particularly the Rohingya, highlights the intersection of ethnic discrimination and human rights violations. Their activism draws international attention to broader issues of ethnic cleansing and genocide.

### Political Opposition and Reform

While grassroots movements lay the groundwork for resistance, organized political opposition is essential for translating popular discontent into political change.

**Formation of Opposition Parties and Coalitions**

In many authoritarian regimes, political opposition faces severe repression, including arrest, exile, or assassination of leaders. Despite these challenges, opposition parties and coalitions often form to challenge the regime through political and electoral processes. In Zimbabwe, the Movement for Democratic Change (MDC) emerged as a significant political force against Robert Mugabe’s Zimbabwe African National Union-Patriotic Front (ZANU-PF).

**Challenges of Achieving Political Reform**

Achieving political reform in authoritarian regimes is fraught with difficulties. These regimes often manipulate electoral processes, suppress dissent, and use state resources to maintain power. The persistence of these tactics can stymie opposition efforts. For instance, in Venezuela, the opposition coalition has struggled against the government’s control over electoral bodies and media, leading to disputed elections and ongoing political stalemate.

Despite these challenges, sustained political opposition can lead to incremental reforms or significant political shifts. The transition from apartheid in South Africa was achieved through a combination of internal opposition by the African National Congress (ANC) and external pressures, resulting in negotiated political reforms and the eventual establishment of a democratic government.

### International Support for Domestic Movements

International support plays a crucial role in bolstering domestic resistance movements. This support can come in various forms, including financial aid, diplomatic pressure, and moral support.

**Financial and Moral Support from Foreign Governments and Organizations**

Foreign governments and international organizations often provide critical financial assistance to domestic resistance movements. This support can help fund organizational activities, provide resources for protests, and sustain media campaigns. For example, during the Cold War, the U.S. government provided support to dissident groups in Eastern Europe, which contributed to the eventual collapse of communist regimes.

**Risks of Foreign Intervention**

While international support can be beneficial, it also carries risks. Foreign intervention can be perceived as external meddling, potentially undermining the legitimacy of domestic movements and giving authoritarian regimes a pretext to crack down on dissidents as "foreign agents." The backlash against Western support for pro-democracy movements in the Middle East, particularly during the Arab Spring, illustrates this delicate balance.

**Case Study: The Role of the International Community in the Arab Spring**

The Arab Spring provides a complex example of international support for domestic movements. In Tunisia, international support helped bolster the transition to democracy. In contrast, in Libya and Syria, foreign intervention led to prolonged conflict and instability. The varied outcomes highlight the importance of tailored and context-sensitive international engagement.

VI. Challenges to Human Rights Advocacy in Authoritarian Contexts
-----------------------------------------------------------------

### Risk of Repression

Advocating for human rights in authoritarian regimes is fraught with significant risks, both for the activists themselves and their associates. Repression is a common response by authoritarian governments aiming to maintain control and silence dissent.

**Repercussions for Activists and Their Families**

Human rights activists in authoritarian regimes often face severe consequences, including harassment, arrest, torture, and even extrajudicial killings. Governments utilize security forces and intelligence agencies to monitor and target activists. The case of Jamal Khashoggi, a Saudi journalist and dissident who was murdered in the Saudi consulate in Istanbul, underscores the extreme lengths to which some regimes will go to silence critics.

Family members of activists are also at risk. Authoritarian regimes frequently employ tactics such as intimidation, harassment, and detention of relatives to pressure activists into ceasing their activities. This tactic not only aims to coerce the primary target but also serves as a broader deterrent to potential activists within the community.

**Strategies for Protection and Safety**

To counter these risks, activists and NGOs adopt various strategies to enhance their protection and safety. Digital security measures, such as encrypted communication tools and secure data storage, are crucial in preventing surveillance and data breaches. Physical safety protocols, including the use of safe houses and strategic relocation, are also vital.

International networks and alliances play a critical role in providing protection. Organizations such as Amnesty International and Human Rights Watch offer platforms for raising awareness about the plight of endangered activists, applying international pressure on repressive governments. The UN Special Rapporteur on the situation of human rights defenders also advocates for the protection of activists worldwide.

### Manipulation of Human Rights Discourse

Authoritarian regimes often manipulate human rights discourse to legitimize their actions and discredit genuine human rights advocacy. This manipulation can take various forms, including co-opting human rights language and presenting themselves as defenders of certain rights.

**Authoritarian Regimes’ Use of Human Rights Rhetoric**

Many authoritarian regimes claim to support human rights by selectively emphasizing certain rights while ignoring or violating others. For instance, a regime might highlight its achievements in economic and social rights, such as poverty reduction or healthcare improvements, to deflect criticism from its violations of civil and political rights.

China, for example, frequently cites its progress in alleviating poverty and improving living standards as evidence of its commitment to human rights. However, this narrative is used to overshadow its widespread suppression of political dissent, freedom of expression, and the treatment of ethnic minorities, such as the Uighurs in Xinjiang.

**Co-opting Human Rights Language for Regime Legitimacy**

Authoritarian regimes also co-opt human rights language to legitimize their rule and discredit opposition. This can involve framing opposition groups as threats to national security or portraying them as foreign agents undermining national sovereignty. By doing so, regimes attempt to justify repressive measures as necessary for protecting the nation and maintaining stability.

The Russian government’s labeling of opposition groups and independent NGOs as “foreign agents” is a pertinent example. This designation not only stigmatizes these groups but also imposes legal and financial burdens that hinder their operations. Similarly, Egypt’s use of counter-terrorism rhetoric to justify crackdowns on political opponents and civil society organizations illustrates this tactic.

Reflections from the Secretary General on the state of human rights in 2023/24
==============================================================================

I never expected the state of human rights to lead me to reference the 1980s sci-fi film _Back to the Future_. Yet here we are. A world spiralling through time, hurtling backwards past the 1948 promise of universal human rights, even as it spins ever faster forwards into a future overtaken by Big Tech and unregulated generative artificial intelligence (AI).

“Authoritarian” practices on the rise
-------------------------------------

In 2023, V-Dem, the political science research centre, found the number of people living in democracies (broadly defined as countries providing rule of law, constraints on the executive by the legislature and the judiciary, and respect for civil liberties) had regressed to **1985 levels**: to levels before the Berlin Wall fell, before Nelson Mandela was released from prison, before the Cold War ended in the hope that a new era for humanity was about to unfold.

That new era was all too brief and today is as good as gone. Evidence of its passing grew in 2023. “Authoritarian” practices and ideas permeated many governments and societies. North to south, east to west, authoritarian policies ate away at freedoms of expression and association, hit out at gender equality, and eroded sexual and reproductive rights.

The underlying public narratives, based in hatred and rooted in fear, encroached on civic space and demonized marginalized individuals and groups, with refugees, migrants and racialized groups bearing the brunt.

The backlash against women’s rights and gender equality intensified in 2023, with many of the past 20 years’ gains under threat.

In Afghanistan, being a woman or a girl has been de facto criminalized. In 2023, the Taliban passed dozens of official decrees aimed at erasing women from public life. Similarly in Iran, the authorities continued their brutal suppression of “Woman, Life, Freedom” protests and issued hate-ridden official statements calling the unveiling of women a “virus”, a “social illness” and a “disorder”.

In the USA, 15 states implemented total bans on abortion, or bans with extremely limited exceptions, bringing disproportionate impacts on Black and other racialized people. In Poland, at least one woman died because the law denied her the abortion services she needed. Uganda adopted a harsh anti-gay law, while social and political leaders in the US also promoted anti-trans narratives, policies and regulations.

Although the world has never been wealthier, 2023 was, as the World Bank called it, “the year of inequality”. In settings as diverse the UK, Hungary and India, defenders of economic and social rights were among the activists most widely targeted. Climate activists were branded “terrorists” for denouncing governments expanding fossil fuel production and investment. Critics of governments’ handlings of the economy in the Middle East, trade unions in Asia-Pacific were silenced and arbitrarily detained, as were those combating corruption in West Africa.

Back to pre-1948?
-----------------

However, in 2023, our metaphorical **time machine** also tossed us much further back than 1985 : **a descent into a hell** whose gates had been bolted closed in **1948**. “Never again”, the world had declared in the aftermath of global warfare with its some 55 million civilian deaths, when faced with the abject horror of a Holocaust that saw the extermination of six million Jews and millions of others.

Yet in 2023, the “never again” moral and legal lessons were torn into a million pieces. Following the horrific crimes perpetrated by Hamas on 7 October 2023 – when over 1,000 people, mostly Israeli civilians, were killed, thousands wounded, and some 245 people taken hostage or captive– Israel instigated a campaign of retaliation that became a campaign of collective punishment. It is a campaign of deliberate, indiscriminate bombings of civilians and civilian infrastructure, of denial of humanitarian assistance and an engineered famine.

By the end of 2023, 21,600 Palestinians, mostly civilians, had been killed in the unrelenting bombardment of Gaza, with thousands more missing, believed buried under the rubble. Much of Gaza’s civilian infrastructure has been obliterated, while nearly 1.9 million Palestinians have been internally displaced and deprived of access to adequate food, water, shelter, sanitation and medical assistance.

To be a Palestinian in Gaza today is to be plunged to a far more violent and destructive version of the 1948 “Nakba” or the “catastrophe”, when more than 750,000 Palestinians were forcibly displaced

For millions the world over, Gaza now symbolizes utter moral failure by many of the architects of the post-World War Two system; their failure to uphold the absolute commitment to universality, our common humanity and to our “never again” commitment. The principles enshrined in the UN Charter, the Geneva Conventions, the Genocide Convention, and international human rights law have been dishonoured. That’s clearest in the case of the Israeli authorities. However, Israel is not alone. The USA too has played a leading part, as have some of Europe’s leaders and the EU leadership. So too have those who continue to send arms to Israel, all who failed to denounce Israel’s relentless violations and those who rejected calls for a ceasefire.

Their conduct exemplifies the double standards that Amnesty International has denounced over many years. Yet, powerful actors have now gone further, demonstrating a willingness to put at risk the entirety of the 1948 rule-based order, stripping down the founding principles of common humanity and universality and thus stripping away our global capacity to prevent the very worst.

It all comes hot on the heels of Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine, a violation of the UN Charter and an undermining of the international rule of law. Russia’s aggression has continued to manifest itself in deliberate attacks against civilians, the killing of thousands, and as widespread destruction of civilian infrastructure, including Ukraine’s grain storage and export facilities.

China too, another permanent member of the UN Security Council, has acted against international law, by protecting the Myanmar military and its unlawful air strikes, through its practices of arrest and torture, and by shielding itself from international scrutiny for the crimes against humanity it continues to commit, including against the Uighur minority.

The future we don’t want
------------------------

In 2023, with the much earlier-than-expected launch of ChatGPT-4 and other generative AI tools, we were also thrust faster into the future. If the tech-related abuses witnessed in 2023 are anything to go by, prospects for our future are chilling indeed.

Tech is enabling pervasive erosions of rights: perpetuating racist policies, enabling spreading misinformation and curtailing freedoms of expression. Big Tech ignored or minimized those harms, even in armed conflicts contexts such as in Ethiopia, Israel and the Occupied Palestinian Territories, Myanmar and Sudan. With the alarming rise in online incitement and other harmful content against both Palestinian and Jewish communities, Europe and the USA also saw marked increases in anti-Muslim and antisemitic hate crimes.

In 2023, States turned increasingly to facial recognition technologies to aid policing of public protests, sporting events and of marginalized communities at large – migrants and refugees in particular. Abusive technologies were relied upon for migration governance and border enforcement, including through border externalization technologies, data analysis software, biometrics and algorithmic decision-making systems.

Despite years of evidence of the human rights violations it enables, spyware remained largely unregulated. In 2023, Amnesty International uncovered use of Pegasus spyware against journalists and civil society activists in Armenia, the Dominican Republic, India and Serbia, while EU-based spyware was sold to states the world over. In response, the European Parliament adopted a resolution in November 2023, criticizing the lack of action to curb abuses by the spyware industry.

However, with tech-outlaws and their rogue technologies left to freely roam the digital Wild West, such human rights violations are likely to escalate in 2024, a landmark electoral year. It is a foreshadowing of a future that is already upon us.

Global solidarity
-----------------

Human rights regressions in 2023 did not go unseen. To the contrary. People around the world have stood up to regression, demonstrating unprecedented global solidarity.

The Israel-Hamas conflict sparked hundreds of protests worldwide, with millions protesting civilian deaths, calling for the release of hostages, demanding a cease fire.

The UN Secretary-General, heads of UN agencies, and humanitarian organizations took unprecedented steps to denounce war crimes committed in southern Israel and Gaza and to call on Israel to respect international law.

The late 2023 UN General Assembly resolutions calling for a ceasefire were adopted with a huge majority while South Africa filed an application before the International Court of Justice, alleging that Israel’s conduct in Gaza violated the 1948 Genocide Convention and insisting on the centrality of the post-World War Two international rule-based system.

2023 saw momentum build towards a global regime for fairer taxation, to help prevent tax evasion and avoidance and mobilize resources for lower-income countries. In November, against the wishes of the richer countries, the UN General Assembly adopted a resolution tabled by the African Group establishing an international committee to draft a UN tax convention by June 2025.

In 2023, there were many people who resisted and disrupted forces pushing the world backwards to the conditions of 1985 and of pre-1948; people who marched and protested against forces that would propel us all into a future not of our design. They too have shaped 2023, against all odds.

I hope that in 2048 – or even 3048 – when diplomats and activists look back at the past year, they will find that there were many, many good people around the world who did all they could. Who stood up and spoke out. For the sake of our common humanity.

Agnès Callamard

Secretary General, Amnesty International

Causes of the rise of authoritarianism There are several causes of the rise of authoritarianism in the world. One of the most significant is the erosion of democracy and the rule of law. In many countries, democratically elected leaders are undermining democratic institutions and processes, such as the judiciary and the media, in order to maintain their grip on power. This is often done through the use of legal mechanisms such as the suppression of free speech, press censorship, and the use of state violence against protesters. Another cause of the rise of authoritarianism is the emergence of strongman leaders who rely on populist rhetoric to gain power. They also tend to project an image of strength and decisiveness, appealing to those who feel that their country is in crisis and needs a strong leader to restore order. The rise of authoritarianism can also be attributed to the increasing polarization of societies, both within countries and between them. This polarization is often fueled by social media and other digital platforms, which can amplify extreme views and create echo chambers that reinforce people's biases. The rise of authoritarianism has led to an increase in human rights violations in many parts of the world. These violations take many forms, including restrictions on freedom of speech and assembly, persecution of minority groups, torture and extrajudicial killings, and violations of the right to due process and a fair trial. One of the most concerning human rights violations is the persecution of minority groups. This includes the persecution of religious and ethnic minorities, as well as the LGBT community. In many countries, these groups are subjected to discrimination, harassment, and violence, often with the tacit or explicit approval of the government. This has led to displacement and refugee crises, as people are forced to flee their homes and seek safety elsewhere. Another form of human rights violation is the use of torture and extrajudicial killings by authoritarian governments. In some countries, torture is used as a means of extracting confessions or punishing political dissidents. Extrajudicial killings, on the other hand, are often used as a means of silencing opposition or eliminating potential threats to the regime. Restrictions on freedom of speech and assembly are also common in authoritarian countries. This often takes the form of censorship of the press and social media, as well as restrictions on the right to protest or engage in political dissent. In some cases, governments have also used violence against protesters, resulting in injuries and deaths. Impact on human rights and democracy The rise of authoritarianism and human rights violations have had a significant impact on human rights and democracy worldwide. In many countries, the erosion of democratic institutions has led to a lack of accountability and transparency, as well as a lack of protection for human rights. This has resulted in a deterioration of the quality of governance and the rule of law, as well as a decline in public trust in institutions. The rise of authoritarianism has also had an impact on international human rights norms and institutions. Many authoritarian governments have sought to undermine international human rights norms and institutions, such as the United Nations Human Rights Council and the International Criminal Court. These two issues are closely related, as authoritarian leaders often use tactics that violate the basic rights of their citizens to consolidate their power. One of the most concerning aspects of this trend is the erosion of freedom of speech and the press. In many countries, journalists and other media professionals face harassment, imprisonment, or even death for reporting on government corruption or other issues that are deemed sensitive. This has a chilling effect on free speech and can make it difficult for citizens to get accurate information about what is happening in their country. Another area of concern is the use of surveillance technology by governments to monitor their citizens. In some cases, this technology is used to crack down on dissent or to identify and punish political opponents. In other cases, it is used to monitor the activities of ordinary citizens, infringing on their privacy and creating a climate of fear and suspicion. There has also been a rise in discrimination and persecution against minority groups, particularly in countries with authoritarian leaders. This can take many forms, including restrictions on religious practices, discrimination based on ethnicity or sexual orientation, or violence against minority groups. These actions are often justified in the name of national security or preserving traditional values, but they have a devastating impact on the lives of those who are targeted. Furthermore, there have been growing concerns about the erosion of democratic institutions in many countries. In some cases, this is due to leaders consolidating their power and weakening the checks and balances that are essential to a healthy democracy. In other cases, it is due to the erosion of public trust in democratic institutions, which can lead to apathy or even hostility towards the democratic process. There are many factors driving this trend towards authoritarianism and human rights violations. One is the growing economic inequality in many countries, which can create a sense of disillusionment and frustration among those who feel left behind by globalization. Another is the rise of populist movements that appeal to nationalist or nativist sentiments, often at the expense of minority groups or other vulnerable populations. In addition, the global community has been slow to respond to these issues, often prioritizing economic or strategic interests over human rights concerns. This has emboldened authoritarian leaders and made it more difficult for those who are fighting for democracy and human rights to get the support they need. To address this trend, it is essential that we prioritize human rights and democracy as key values in our foreign policy and global institutions. This means supporting civil society organizations, independent media, and other groups that are working to promote human rights and democracy. It also means holding governments accountable for their actions and using diplomatic and economic tools to pressure them to change course when they violate human rights. In addition, we must address the root causes of authoritarianism and human rights violations, including economic inequality, political polarization, and social exclusion. Authoritarianism Authoritarianism, in politics and government, the blind submission to authority and the repression of individual freedom of thought and action. Authoritarian regimes are systems of government that have no established mechanism for the transfer of executive power and do not afford their citizens civil liberties or political rights. Power is concentrated in the hands of a single leader or a small elite, whose decisions are taken without regard for the will of the people. The term authoritarianism is often used to denote any form of government that is not democratic, but studies have demonstrated that there is a great deal of variation in authoritarian rule. One of the most pressing human rights issues in Libya is the use of arbitrary detention by various armed groups and authorities. Thousands of people are being held in detention centers throughout the country without trial, often in horrific conditions. Reports of torture, forced disappearances, and extrajudicial killings are widespread, with many detainees subjected to physical and psychological abuse. In addition, women and children are often held in the same facilities as men, further exacerbating the risks of abuse and violence. The situation for refugees and migrants in Libya is similarly dire. The country is a key transit point for people attempting to reach Europe, and many are detained upon arrival and subjected to various forms of abuse. Reports suggest that migrants are often held in overcrowded and unsanitary conditions, with little access to food, water, or medical care. They are also frequently subjected to extortion, rape, and other forms of violence by their captors. RESULTS Human rights violations are actions that infringe upon the basic rights and freedoms that every person is entitled to. These rights include the right to life, liberty, and security of person, freedom from torture and slavery, freedom of expression and religion, and the right to fair and equal treatment under the law. When these rights are violated, it can have severe consequences for individuals and communities, and can often lead to long-term damage to society as a whole. One of the most severe human rights violations is torture. Torture involves intentionally inflicting severe physical or psychological pain on someone to extract information, intimidate or punish them. This practice is illegal under international law, but it is still carried out in many countries around the world. Victims of torture often suffer from physical and emotional scars that can last a lifetime, and their rights to due process and a fair trial are often violated. © 2023 IJNRD | Volume 8, Issue 5 May 2023 | ISSN: 2456-4184 | IJNRD.ORG IJNRD2305319 International Journal of Novel Research and Development (www.ijnrd.org) d134 Another form of human rights violations is human trafficking, which involves the exploitation of people for profit. This can take many forms, including forced labor, sex trafficking, and child labor. Victims of human trafficking are often subjected to brutal conditions, with little or no pay, and are often denied basic human rights like freedom of movement and communication. The psychological trauma and physical abuse they experience can have lasting effects on their health and well-being. In many countries, discrimination based on race, ethnicity, gender, or sexual orientation is a widespread problem, leading to serious human rights violations. Discrimination can take many forms, from hate speech to systemic exclusion from opportunities and resources. This can lead to the marginalization of certain groups, which in turn can lead to social and economic inequality. Violence against women is another significant human rights issue. This can take many forms, from domestic violence to sexual assault and harassment. Women are often subjected to violence simply because of their gender, which violates their basic human rights and can have serious long-term consequences for their physical and mental health. Finally, the denial of basic human rights, such as freedom of expression and assembly, can have serious consequences for democracy and civil society. When people are denied the right to speak out and organize peacefully, it can lead to the suppression of dissent and the perpetuation of authoritarian regimes. In conclusion, human rights violations are a serious problem that affects people around the world. These violations take many forms, from torture and human trafficking to discrimination and violence against women. It is the responsibility of governments and individuals to protect and promote human rights, and to ensure that everyone has access to the basic freedoms and protections they deserve. CONCLUSION The past few years have seen a rise in authoritarianism in various parts of the world, accompanied by an increase in human rights violations. This trend is concerning as it undermines democracy, individual freedoms, and the rule of law. In this analysis, we will examine the causes of this rise in authoritarianism and the resulting human rights violations. One factor contributing to the rise of authoritarianism is economic inequality. The widening gap between the rich and the poor has led to a sense of disillusionment among the working class, who feel that their governments are not addressing their needs. This has created an opportunity for populist leaders to exploit this sentiment, often by scapegoating minority groups and promising simple solutions to complex problems. Populism has been on the rise in countries such as Hungary, Turkey, and Brazil, where leaders have been elected on platforms that promise to protect the interests of the working class. Another factor contributing to the rise of authoritarianism is the erosion of democratic institutions. In many countries, the independence of the judiciary, the free press, and civil society has been compromised. This has enabled authoritarian leaders to consolidate their power, often by circumventing the constitutional checks and balances that exist in democracies. In Hungary, for example, the government has taken control of the judiciary, the media, and civil society, thereby undermining the country's democracy. The rise of authoritarianism has also been facilitated by the increasing polarization of politics. The proliferation of social media platforms has made it easier for people to consume news that confirms their biases, leading to echo chambers that reinforce extreme views. This has led to a breakdown in dialogue between different groups, with each side demonizing the other.
